title: Invoking a Tool from the Client
concept: Sending a tool call request with arguments and handling the structured response.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about discovering what a server can do. What is this step called?

options.text: Sending a ping to check the server's CPU usage
Asking the server to list its available capabilities and tools
Downloading the entire source code of the server

exercises.explain: Before invoking a tool, the client discovers available tools and capabilities by calling the list capabilities method (like 'listTools') on the connected server.

cards.title: From Listing to Action
cards.body: Knowing what tools a server has is like reading a restaurant menu. But reading isn't eating! To actually get things done, your client needs to know how to place an order by sending arguments and handling the response.

cards.title: What is Tool Invocation?
cards.body: Invoking a tool means sending a formal request from the client to the server, specifying the tool's name and the exact argument values it expects. The server executes the function and returns a structured response, typically containing text, images, or resource references.

cards.title: The Burger Order Analogy
cards.body: Think of the client like a customer ordering a custom burger. The server is the chef. Instead of just pointing, the client fills out a specific order slip (the tool arguments) and hand-delivers it. The chef cooks the burger and hands back a structured tray (the tool result) containing the food.

cards.title: A Client-to-Server Call
cards.body: If a server offers a tool named 'fetch_weather' that takes a 'city' argument, the client sends a request object like: { name: 'fetch_weather', arguments: { city: 'London' } }. The server responds with structured text detailing the temperature and conditions.

cards.title: Where Does the Work Happen?
cards.body: Many beginners think that invoking a tool means the client executes the code. In reality, the client only sends the request; the server does all the heavy lifting and code execution, then sends back the clean results.

cards.title: Request vs. Response
cards.body: Understanding the difference between what goes out to the server and what comes back to the client.

left.text: Tool name + key-value arguments sent from client to server.

right.text: Structured list of content blocks (text/images) returned to client.

exercises.prompt: How does the server package the results of a tool execution back to the client?

options.text: A raw HTML webpage containing ads
A structured object containing content blocks like text or images
A direct link to download a Python execution script
A simple yes or no boolean flag

exercises.explain: The response from an MCP tool call returns a structured list of content blocks, which can be text, images, or other media types, allowing the client to safely parse the output.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To invoke a tool, the client can just send a raw text message like 'hey server run the calculator' to the server.
exercises.explain: Clients and servers communicate using structured messages (like JSON-RPC). Sending raw, unformatted text strings would cause parsing errors.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how we supply parameters to an MCP tool.
exercises.sentence: An MCP client invokes a tool by sending its name along with the structured arguments required by the server.
exercises.explain: When calling a tool, we must supply the required arguments as a structured object so the server knows exactly what parameters to run the tool with.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following represents a standard structured response returned by an MCP server after a tool invocation?
exercises.explain: Option B is the correct AI-style representation of an MCP tool call response, formatted with a structured 'content' array containing a text block. Option A is an HTTP redirect, and Option C is a raw SQL query.

exercises.prompt: Imagine your MCP client wants to call a tool named 'calculate_tax' on a server. What must the client send in its request, and what structure does it expect in return?

options.text: A request object with the tool's name and arguments; the server returns a structured response containing a 'content' array.
A raw text string with the tool name; the server returns a simple, unformatted stream of console logs.
An empty ping request; the server automatically detects what inputs to use based on the client's past history.
A binary file upload; the server returns an encrypted database connection string.

exercises.explain: To invoke a tool, the client must send a request with both the tool's name and its required input arguments. If successful, the server replies with a structured response that contains a 'content' array, which holds the text, image, or resource data generated by the tool.
